note: Verbatim phrases of the locked narration, in spoken order, split at the script's <break> tags and sentence pauses (never rewritten, summarized, or reordered; pronunciation spellings kept exactly as written in the script, e.g. 'B-T one-oh-three-ninety-three', dollar amounts spelled out). Block windows are MEASURED onsets, not the nominal formula: Sigma(ffprobe per-file audio + 0.4) = 207.868s, which overshoots the ffprobe-probed master (207.491s) by 0.377s (> 0.3 tolerance) because the concatenated MP3 frames decode ~0.38s shorter than ffprobe's per-file duration sum. Onsets were therefore measured by normalized cross-correlation of each WC-PB-05-sNN.mp3 (first 1.5s template, 8 kHz mono PCM) against the joined master; all 11 blocks matched at score >= 0.9999, and the measured onsets equal cumulative Sigma(decoded audio + 0.4), confirming the master was joined with exact 0.4s gaps. Master ffprobe duration 207.491s.

phrases.text: Start on Payments Reconciliation.
This is the settlement ledger, one row per batch.
Expected is what the day should have banked.
Settled is what the processor actually deposited.
Variance is the gap between the two.
Read those three columns and the whole page makes sense.
Now the status column.
Fort Lauderdale and Orlando Central settled exactly what they expected, so they read balanced.
Miami North came up a hundred sixty dollars short:
expected fourteen thousand, eight hundred eighty,
settled fourteen thousand, seven hundred twenty.
That nonzero gap flags it for review.
The review row is the one you work.
With more than a few batches, narrow the list.
Search by batch, location, or status,
or sort straight on the variance column to pull the gaps to the top.
Nothing here touches a batch.
You're only deciding which one to chase.
Top-right of the panel, find Resolve batch.
That's the control a supervisor uses to close out a review batch.
You locate it and stop.
Working it, and running any refund, happens in production with the right role.
Never from this demo.
Before you leave the ledger, decide what to chase.
A settlement gap is usually one of two things at that site:
the drawer came up over or short, or cash was paid out and never banked.
Carry the location to both checks.
There's no date column here, so location is the join,
and a shift number ties a payout back to its drawer.
First the drawer. In Sales, open Shifts.
Every drawer session lists out:
shift number, location, terminal,
when it opened and closed, and the opening and closing drawer.
Those last two are where an over or short shows up for the day.
Filter to the batch's location and read them.
One caution from today's capture.
The list came back empty with a fetch error, and the total-shifts count was blank.
Don't read that as no shifts.
Confirm the data actually loaded before you tell anyone the drawer was clean.
Now the cash side. Open Payouts.
Each row shows the location, payout ID, shift number, cashier, terminal, vendor, and amount.
A payout links to a drawer by its shift number,
so cash pulled for a vendor explains part of a gap with nothing actually missing.
Read the payout status card.
It says approval and dispute status aren't tracked here;
payouts records completed disbursements only.
So this page confirms what went out, not whether it was approved.
And today it too returned the fetch error, so treat the rows as unconfirmed until they load.
Now build the ticket.
Name the batch, B-T one-oh-three-ninety-three, and the location, Miami North.
Give the three numbers:
expected fourteen thousand, eight hundred eighty;
settled fourteen thousand, seven hundred twenty;
variance a hundred sixty.
Mark it review, and say whether a drawer figure or a payout explains the gap.
You never resolve the batch, and you never run a refund from demo.
So that's the playbook.
Read the ledger, find the review batch by its status and its variance,
locate Resolve batch without touching it,
then cross-check the location's shifts and payouts to explain the gap.
Escalate with the batch and the amounts.
You moved nothing.
You found where the money went and passed it on.
